title: Suggestions007 Source Reading Audit
goal: Audit the widened reading mode and embedded spoiler-gated solution experience against suggestions007.md using a guided Playwright session with reproducible screenshots and product-level observations.
intent: Judge whether the explorer now supports comfortable deep inspection, visible but controlled spoiler access, honest attribution, and readable multi-language source study without breaking the field-guide-first browsing flow.
reviewQuestions: Does the selected problem become materially easier to read when widened?
Are spoiler solutions clearly available while still remaining closed by default?
Does each revealed solution make the repository, original file, and upstream source obvious enough to trust?
Does the solution layer feel educational and coherent instead of like a pasted code dump?
reminders: Do not confuse functional code loading with a good reading experience; inspect width, hierarchy, and code legibility.
When a spoiler works, still check whether the collapsed state communicates enough before the user opens it.
Look around the surrounding detail pane so the source area does not silently make the rest of the inspection flow worse.
baseline: Run the stable acceptance suite first.
Use screenshots to judge readability, not only DOM presence.
Record observations tied to specific requirements from suggestions007.md.

steps.title: Audit the default non-spoiling detail state
steps.intent: Check that the explorer still reads like a selection tool before any explicit deep-inspection action.
steps.focus: Collapsed solution workspace, visible availability of implementations, and overall detail-pane calmness.
lookAround: Can I tell that solutions exist without seeing code yet?
Does the detail pane still prioritize explanation over implementation by default?
Do the visible solution summaries feel like a natural extension of repository support?
reviewQuestions: Does the page still preserve the field-guide-first philosophy in its default state?
steps.observation: The default selected-problem view should advertise embedded solutions without collapsing into a spoiler-heavy reading surface.

steps.title: Audit the widened reading mode
steps.intent: Check whether widening the inspection surface materially improves readability and inspection comfort.
steps.focus: Expanded detail width, section breathing room, and preservation of orientation within the workspace.
lookAround: Do paragraphs, repository sections, and spoiler summaries feel less cramped now?
Can I still tell where I am in relation to the catalog after widening?
Does the transition feel explicit and reversible instead of awkward?
reviewQuestions: Would I actually choose this mode for deeper inspection rather than ignoring it?
steps.observation: The widened mode should feel like a real reading upgrade rather than a cosmetic panel resize.

steps.title: Audit collapsed spoiler summaries
steps.intent: Check whether the closed spoiler state communicates language coverage and source availability clearly enough.
steps.focus: Inline solution cards, language switching, and spoiler labels before any code is revealed.
lookAround: Do the card summaries show repository identity, language, and original path clearly?
Is the spoiler label direct enough about revealing implementations?
Would a careful user know they are about to reveal full source code?
reviewQuestions: Does the collapsed solution layer inform without spoiling?
steps.observation: The collapsed state should be visible and informative, not vague or accidental.

steps.title: Audit revealed inline source
steps.intent: Check whether opening a spoiler reveals a readable reviewed implementation with complete attribution.
steps.focus: Repository identity, original file path, upstream links, attribution note, and reviewed code readability.
lookAround: Is repository attribution obvious inside the reveal itself, not just in the surrounding card?
Do the reader comments feel helpful instead of noisy?
Does the code block have enough width and visual stability to study comfortably?
reviewQuestions: Would I trust this reveal as an honest attributed educational adaptation of upstream code?
steps.observation: The revealed source should read like an attributed reviewed study surface, not a code dump.

steps.title: Audit the focused solution workspace
steps.intent: Check whether the dedicated solution workspace still works for deeper comparison across languages and repositories.
steps.focus: Modal inspection workflow, language order, source switching, annotations, and attribution panel clarity.
lookAround: Does this focused mode feel like a natural deeper extension of the inline solution layer?
Are language tabs and source tabs readable rather than messy?
Do annotations and supporting writeups still stay subordinate to the code they explain?
reviewQuestions: Does the focused workspace earn its existence beyond the inline spoiler experience?
steps.observation: The focused workspace should make cross-language or cross-repository inspection easier, not merely duplicate the inline view.
